serving.natural_language_portion: One medium fresh plum weighing approximately 66 g is a typical single-serve portion.

nutrient_source.title: Plum, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for fresh raw plum (cr_id F04010032). Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central NDB 09279 (Plums, raw) confirmed alignment for core macros and extended micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plum GI of 40 (glucose reference = 100) is reported directly in Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56), table entry for plum. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 7.54) / 100 ≈ 3.0, based on a 66 g serving containing approximately 7.54 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium; potassium 157 mg/100 g supports vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Fresh plum is sodium-free and provides potassium (~157 mg/100 g), which supports blood pressure regulation; individuals with hypertension may consume 2–3 plums per day as part of a DASH-aligned diet without concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40); sugar modulated by intact fruit matrix and 1.4 g/100 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh plum has a low glycaemic index of approximately 40 and a glycaemic load of ~3 per medium fruit; individuals with type 2 diabetes may include 1–2 plums per serving, monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding dried plum products with concentrated sugars (~38 g/100 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g); zero cholesterol; anthocyanins and polyphenols with antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh plum contains negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and provides anthocyanins and polyphenols that reduce oxidative LDL modification; regular consumption of whole fresh plums is consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant polyphenols and vitamin C present; however fibre content below 3 g/100 g threshold and no omega-3 fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh plum provides vitamin C (9.5 mg/100 g), anthocyanins, and polyphenols that may reduce oxidative stress relevant to RA, but lacks omega-3 fatty acids and has insufficient fibre to qualify as a strong anti-inflammatory food; prefer fresh over preserved or dried plum products which may contain high sodium or concentrated sugars.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plum (Prunus domestica and related species including Prunus salicina) is a round stone fruit belonging to the Rosaceae family, with smooth, thin skin ranging in colour from deep purple and red to yellow and green depending on variety, enclosing sweet-tart, juicy flesh surrounding a hard central stone pit. Fresh plums require no preparation beyond washing and are consumed raw as a snack or dessert fruit; they may also be halved and stoned for use in fruit salads, compotes, jams, chutneys, baked goods such as tarts and crumbles, and savoury sauces. When fully ripe, the flesh is soft, succulent, and fragrant, with a balance of sweetness and mild tartness; underripe plums are firmer and more astringent. Plums are cultivated across temperate regions of Europe, Asia, and North America, with European varieties (Prunus domestica) tending toward oval shapes and blue-purple skin, while Japanese varieties (Prunus salicina) are typically rounder with red or yellow skin and are more widely available in Asian markets including Singapore. Per 100 g, fresh plum provides approximately 46 kcal, 11.4 g total carbohydrate (of which 9.9 g is naturally occurring sugar), 1.4 g dietary fibre, 0.7 g protein, and only 0.28 g total fat (of which 0.02 g is saturated fat). Sodium content is zero and potassium is approximately 157 mg/100 g. The fruit has a low glycaemic index of approximately 40 (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when consumed in moderate portions of 1–2 medium plums. The intact fruit matrix, combined with 1.4 g/100 g dietary fibre, slows gastric emptying and attenuates the postprandial glucose response relative to plum juice or dried plum products. Plums are rich in anthocyanins, chlorogenic acid, and other polyphenols concentrated in the skin, as well as vitamin C (9.5 mg/100 g) and vitamin A (17 mcg RAE/100 g), contributing antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties. They are naturally free of gluten, dairy, and cholesterol, and are suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher diets. Clinically, plums are appropriate for hypertension management due to zero sodium and meaningful potassium content supporting vasodilation, and support cardiovascular health through negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and antioxidant phytochemicals that may reduce oxidative LDL modification. Dried plums (prunes) have a substantially different nutritional profile with concentrated sugars (~38 g/100 g) and a higher glycaemic impact, and must not be substituted for fresh plums in clinical dietary guidance.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F04010032, confidence 0.95), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central NDB 09279 (Plums, raw). GI value sourced from peer-reviewed Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr). All values are consistent across sources.
notes: Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central which reports 0 mg sodium for raw plum. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed negligible for raw fresh fruit per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as plum is a plant food containing no animal-derived cobalamin. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as plum is a non-animal, non-fortified food. Selenium is 0.0 mcg — confirmed trace/below detection threshold per USDA FoodData Central NDB 09279. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero for a plant food. Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated fat (0.13 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.04 g), and extended micronutrients (potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09279 as a secondary cross-reference where HPB did not report these fields. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry from the input record has been retained as an additional_condition with concern_level moderate and a balanced clinical note; the original input's suitable=false flag was inconsistent with the reason text describing positive antioxidant properties and has been resolved by assigning moderate concern with nuanced guidance. Dried plums (prunes) have a substantially different nutritional profile and must not be confused with fresh plum in dietary guidance.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Expanded natural_language_description to address Q6 feedback regarding insufficient preparation/sensory/clinical/cultural coverage.

Iteration 3: Further expanded natural_language_description to explicitly cover preparation methods (raw consumption, cooking applications such as compotes, jams, tarts, crumbles, savoury sauces), detailed sensory profile (texture at different ripeness stages, aroma, taste balance), cultural and geographic context distinguishing European Prunus domestica from Japanese Prunus salicina varieties and their availability in Asian markets, mechanistic explanation of fibre's role in attenuating postprandial glucose response, specific phytochemical identification (anthocyanins, chlorogenic acid), and explicit clinical linkage for all three required health conditions. Description now approximately 2,100 characters, well above the 200-character minimum and substantively rich for RAG/LLM use.
